data.title: Russian strikes kill 2 in Kyiv while diplomatic efforts to end Ukraine war gather pace
data.description:
Russian drone and missile attacks on the Ukrainian capital, Kyiv, killed at least two people in the early hours of Saturday, local officials said.
The attacks came just before a second round of peace negotiations was set to begin, as a renewed US-led push to end the war gathers steam this week.
Writing on its official Telegram channel, the Kyiv City Military Administration said two people were killed in the strikes.
Mayor Vitali Klitschko said 29 people were wounded, noting that falling debris from intercepted Russian drones hit residential buildings. He also said the western part of Kyiv had lost power.
The latest assault on Kyiv came as Ukrainian peace negotiators are due to meet their US counterparts in America this weekend, according to an official in Ukraine’s presidential administration who spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitivity of the talks.
A US delegation is then expected to travel to Moscow for talks with Russian President Vladimir Putin in the second half of next week.

US President Donald Trump last week released a plan for ending the nearly four-year war. The 28-point proposal heavily favoured Russia, prompting Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to quickly engage with American negotiators.
European leaders, fearing for their own future in the face of Russian aggression, scrambled to steer the negotiations towards accommodating their concerns.
Trump said on Tuesday that his plan to end the war had been “fine-tuned” and that he is sending envoy Steve Witkoff to Russia to meet with Putin and Army Secretary Dan Driscoll to meet with Ukrainian officials.
Trump suggested he could eventually meet with Putin and Zelensky, but not until further progress has been made in negotiations.


Zelensky announced on Friday the resignation of his powerful chief of staff, Andrii Yermak, who was also the country’s lead negotiator in talks with the US, after anti-corruption investigators searched Yermak’s residence.
The unprecedented search at the heart of Ukraine’s government was a blow to the Ukrainian leader, risking the disruption of his negotiating strategy at a time when Kyiv is under intense US pressure to sign a peace deal.

data.title: Chinese man chases TV crew for 100km to show motorcycle skills, now owns US$99 million firm
data.description:
From chasing a TV crew for 100km to showcase his motorcycle skills, a once-poor young man from central China has built a thriving motorcycle empire, raking in 700 million (US$100 million) yuan a year.
Born into a rural family in Hunan province, Zhang Xue grew up in a shabby home made of bricks and wooden boards with his younger sister after his parents divorced.
During one of her visits, his mother gave him a bicycle, sparking his lifelong love for two-wheeled vehicles.

After finishing secondary school, Zhang began work as an apprentice at a motorcycle shop, learning repairs and riding skills.
Despite his passion for motorcycles, his financial struggles kept him from pursuing formal training.
At the age of 19, Zhang saw an opportunity when the Evening programme crew from Hunan TV came to town to film. He approached them and asked if he could showcase his skills.

“Your programme has a wide audience. If I get exposure, I might have a chance to join a professional motorcycle team. Once I am in, I can do anything, fix bikes, cook and clean,” he told them.
The reporters politely declined his request.
Undeterred, Zhang rode his old motorcycle, chasing the crew’s car for three hours through rain and over rugged mountain roads, covering 100km. On the muddy paths, he drifted, sped and pushed his limits.
Moved by his persistence but concerned for his safety, the crew finally stopped to film.

An exhausted Zhang stumbled and fell several times during his performance.
Afterwards, he texted the crew asking for another chance.
When they returned, Zhang performed daring stunts, including wheelies, riding while lying down and controlling the wheel with his hands until his motorcycle broke down.
With a smile, he said to the camera: “If you have a dream, chase it. Because of my bravery, my life is more exciting.”

The programme aired and Zhang’s determination caught the attention of a professional motorcycle team for which he became both a stunt rider and a mechanic.
Two years later, he joined a motorcycle company, gradually rising to the position of engineer.
In 2013, Zhang moved to Chongqing and joined a local motorcycle team to help develop vehicles.
By 2017, he had co-founded Kove Motorcycles, which specialises in high-performance racing bikes.
However, in 2024, after business disagreements, Zhang left Kove to create his own brand, Zhang Xue Motorcycles.
Known for its self-developed engines and exceptional value, Zhang Xue Motorcycles quickly made waves in the industry. The 500RR, their flagship model, sold more than 10,000 units in just four months.

By September, the company reportedly hit 370 million yuan (US$52 million) in revenue, a number that is projected to reach 700 million yuan by 2025.
“My resources and talents may not be the best, but when my effort is 10 times greater than others, success will be mine,” Zhang said.
Now, the 38-year-old is determined to make Zhang Xue Motorcycles a world-class brand.
One online observer said: “When young Zhang chased the TV crew, his eyes were full of ambition. Now, he has caught up with his dream.”
While another added: “Zhang’s success was not luck. It was inevitable. He has action, execution and creativity, with persistence and determination that is beyond the ordinary.”

data.title: How Daniel Craig has evolved detective Benoit Blanc in the Knives Out series
data.description:
The greatest mystery in Rian Johnson’s Knives Out films might be Benoit Blanc.
Over the course of three films, Johnson and Daniel Craig have stingily dropped clues to Blanc’s past and personal life. Since Blanc introduced himself in Knives Out as “a respectful, quiet, passive observer … of the truth”, following the breadcrumbs has been a sport of its own.
There are, for instance, the vague, offhand references to cases he has cracked before: something with a tennis champion, another with a ballet dancer and, in the latest chapter, Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery, we hear about something dastardly at the Kentucky Derby that he solved.
Blanc has been profiled in The New Yorker and was a guest on The View. He appears to live with Hugh Grant. He dislikes the board game Clue.
In Wake Up Dead Man, which is showing at cinemas and will stream on Netflix on December 12, Blanc takes up the case of a priest (Josh Brolin) who dies mysteriously in the middle of a church service.
Of the film’s many delights – among them, Josh O’Connor’s co-leading performance as a priest under suspicion and a cast of parishioners including Andrew Scott, Jeremy Renner and Glenn Close – is seeing Craig continue to find new little wrinkles to Blanc.
Rather than being set in stone, Blanc has evolved. Take that accent. The first script, Johnson recalls, described “the slightest hint of a Southern lilt”. But Craig, taking inspiration from Tennessee Williams and Shelby Foote, pushed the accent closer to, as Chris Evans’ character says in Knives Out, a “Kentucky-fried Foghorn Leghorn drawl”. In Glass Onion, he laid it on even thicker, part of a scheme revealed only later in the film.


“My biggest fear was that it would devolve,” Craig says, chuckling. “If it ever becomes pastiche, it’s like, ‘Whoa, let’s get out of here.’ God knows I’m not comparing myself to Gene Wilder, but the way Gene Wilder did comedy was: it’s all through truth. As long as you’re as truthful as you can get in that situation, the funny comes out.”
Craig very nearly missed out on Benoit Blanc. He was initially unavailable for Knives Out due to production on No Time to Die. Johnson sought other possible actors.
“It was literally five weeks later we were shooting. We didn’t think you were available,” Johnson says. “Then something happened where suddenly you guys got delayed for three months and we had a window.”
“I read it and I was shocked that someone would send this to me,” Craig says. “Overjoyed. I saw it from the [start]. I read it and I visualised it. It’s a testament to his writing. I mean, come on. Benoit Blanc.”
In forming the role, Craig took inspiration from Jacques Tati’s debonair but bumbling Monsieur Hulot and Cary Grant’s elegant panache in To Catch a Thief. He combed through out-of-print books of Southern expressions.
Along the way, Craig has improvised some of Blanc’s best expressions. In Wake Up Dead Man, he suddenly blurts out, as if moved by the swelling whodunnit high jinks: “Scooby Dooby Doo!” A sip of Jeremy Renner-sponsored hot sauce in Glass Onion led to the infamous “Halle Berry!”
“All of the best lines in there are things Daniel just brings,” says Johnson. “He says, ‘What about this?’ and I start laughing. And it’s the best line in the movie.”
“I have a security team and there’s a guy that says it,” Craig says of the etymology of “Halle Berry”. “I stole it. I said, ‘Can I have that?’ and he said, ‘Yep.’”

For Craig and Johnson, Blanc has been an ongoing conversation. Wake Up Dead Man, the most sincere of the three mysteries, deals significantly with matters of faith and religion.
The two worked to sharpen Blanc’s perspective. In the film, he declares himself “a proud heretic. I kneel at the altar of the rational.”
Then there are the ornate flourishes of dialogue Johnson pens for Blanc. Modelled on Agatha Christie’s Hercule Poirot, Blanc is the knowing product of a rich literary tradition, dusted off for modern times. In contemporary satires, Blanc is the retro linchpin.
That means Craig delivering lines like “I suspect foul play” silhouetted against a fireplace, and vowing to uncover “what this flock of wicked wolves is hiding” while framed in a stained-glass window.
“Delicious,” Craig says with a grin.
It’s ironic that, on the heels of their own experiences with iconic film series, Johnson and Craig have built a franchise all their own. Johnson released Knives Out two years after the much-debated Star Wars: The Last Jedi. As he exited the James Bond films, Craig donned the suit of another justice-seeker, albeit one with much different swimwear.

“I don’t think either of us really thought about it that way,” Johnson says. “It’s just been making one movie after another, just trying to keep it challenging and fresh for ourselves. It feels almost accidental that suddenly we’ve made three. It definitely wasn’t setting out to build, God forbid, the filthiest word in the universe: IP. We’re just trying to make movies.”
“I’ve been doing this for long enough that as soon as you start counting your chickens on a job, it’s all over,” adds Craig.
Yet it is now possible, especially as the two contemplate a fourth Knives Out film, that there are young film-goers who know Craig more as Benoit Blanc than they do that other B name.
If Johnson and Craig do keep Knives Out going, even as a two-film deal with Netflix concludes, it will allow Johnson the chance to restock his whodunnit cupboard. But it will just as surely offer the opportunity to relaunch and play with Blanc.
“I really love, in my mystery detectives, for them to be kind of enigmas. It pointedly doesn’t work when you start digging into backstory with the detective,” says Johnson.
“That’s always kind of boring because character is only revealed through action, and the action of a detective is such a strong thing. He’s there to solve the case.”

data.title: Why this Aids charity’s Hong Kong art show smells of resilience
data.description:
Victor clearly recalls the day 10 years ago that changed his life.
It was a Sunday, and he had gone to Hong Kong charity Aids Concern, where he volunteers, to have a rapid antibody test for human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), an infection that attacks the body’s immune system.
For weeks, he had been experiencing diarrhoea that his friends told him was probably a severe case of food poisoning. Victor’s instincts told him it was something more serious.
His fears were amplified because he had been engaging in high-risk sexual behaviour – unprotected sex with numerous partners – following a painful break-up with his boyfriend that left him feeling empty.

He turned to dating apps to ease the loneliness, with one hook-up leaving him particularly worried that he might have caught a disease. His instincts proved correct.
“I remember staring at the test and watching it turn positive,” says Victor, 35, who moved to Hong Kong from mainland China with his family when he was 10. “It was a real shock – it felt like everything had suddenly become muted.”
What followed were bouts of depression and isolation. He could not tell his conservative parents about his diagnosis – he had not come out to them about his sexuality. He only told his sister, his only sibling.
“It was a really dark time.”
Fast forward, and Victor is in a happier place. He is on medication – one pill a day – has a better understanding of his identity, and practises self-love, teaching yoga and Pilates, which helps with his own mental health.
He is also one of 15 people who have contributed to “Emanate”, an exhibition by Aids Concern, the first charitable organisation in Hong Kong dedicated to HIV/Aids prevention, community health and the promotion of diverse sexual health.
Taking place at Wyndham Social in Central until December 7, the exhibition features bespoke room fragrances and diffusion stone installations created by people living with HIV, carers, counsellors and those working in the medical field.
Each artwork – installations made with meaningful objects – tells the creators’ story of resilience, support and transformation.
Take the work of King Nam, a heterosexual man in his seventies whose piece, Tea Arts, Network, includes the smartphone that was given to him by his family after he was hospitalised so he could stay in touch with them.
“Yet once the diagnosis was revealed, their attitude flipped 180 degrees, and nearly all communication ceased. I could not accept it; life plunged into absolute despair,” he writes in a note accompanying his piece.
“My rebirth began with a sudden visit from a group of healthcare workers and social workers, reminding me that humanity still holds warmth.
“I later joined many brain-health activities and medication lectures organised by Aids Concern and other groups, gradually guiding me back to a healthy path in life. I sincerely thank Aids Concern for this chance to release the depression buried in my heart for years.”
In the piece Autumn Comes and Goes, Amber, the primary carer for her HIV-positive parents – her father recently died – opens up about the struggles carers face.
“HIV casts a shadow over the family from the start. Outsiders misunderstand, gaze upon us with wary eyes. People say caring for elders is a blessing. As my parents’ sole support, I crave emotional and practical aid to sustain my strength.”

Bonnie Wong, a Hong Kong doctor specialising in infectious diseases, is also part of the exhibition, with her piece Boundless.
It explores the groundbreaking advances in HIV treatment over the past 40 years and the societal shift brought by the evidence-based principle “Undetectable = Untransmittable” (U=U), meaning that if a person with HIV has the virus suppressed to the point that their viral load is undetectable, they cannot transmit it.
Aids Concern CEO Jerome Yau says the exhibition is helping to raise much-needed awareness.
HIV remains a major global public health issue, having so far claimed 44 million lives, according to the World Health Organization.
If left untreated, HIV can lead to Aids (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome), the most severe stage of HIV. There is no vaccine to prevent HIV infection and no cure for HIV/Aids.
But unlike in the 1980s and ’90s – the epidemic was first clinically observed in 1981, and there was no effective treatment until 1997 – an HIV diagnosis is no longer a death sentence.
Access to effective HIV prevention, diagnosis, treatment (antiretroviral drugs) and care means an HIV infection has become a manageable chronic health condition, enabling those living with HIV to lead long and healthy lives.
In 2024, new HIV infections in Hong Kong dropped to a record low of 365, with most infections in the 20 to 75 age range and most new infections occurring in men.
The prevalence of HIV in Hong Kong’s general public is also low, Yau adds, coming in at 0.1 per cent, well below the global average of 0.6 per cent.
Yau says that while the numbers are encouraging, the city is lacking when it comes to sexuality education. Sexual contact is the main mode of transmission.
“The best practice is the comprehensive sexuality education frameworks developed by numerous UN agencies,” he says. “It’s a robust framework containing age-appropriate materials, so you need to start teaching early on and build from there.”

Those who test positive for the virus take medication for life, with life expectancy the same as non-infected peers. There is still no cure.
“We should not become complacent, especially if we want to reach the goal of ending HIV as a public health threat by achieving zero new infections by 2030, which is a worldwide goal,” Yau says.
Yau says the younger generation is wrong if they see Aids and HIV as an 1980s issue. “It’s still around and a public health threat. And if you’re not paying attention, then who knows what’s going to happen?”
He adds that one of the biggest obstacles is societal stigma.
“This leads people living with HIV to avoid or deny their condition out of fear, directly affecting treatment outcomes and daily life.
“In addition to focusing resources on high-risk groups, we also adopt an affirmative approach in serving the wider community, aiming to eliminate misunderstanding and discrimination towards people living with HIV – so that everyone can enjoy good sexual and mental health.”
Victor has felt the pain of prejudice. Fear prevented him from seeking treatment from a public hospital for post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), a course of anti-HIV medications taken to prevent infection after a recent high-risk exposure. PEP must be started within 72 hours and taken for 28 consecutive days to be most effective.
The reason? He feared judgment from medical staff.
The exhibition also features a room-spray workshop and a tea ceremony on December 6.
Also on show are works by Hong Kong poet Lut Ming, artist Trevor Yeung, and Hong Kong-born Indian-Filipino artist Sharu Binnong Sikdar, whose piece incorporates pebbles.

“When you throw a pebble into a river, it creates a ripple effect,” Sikdar says. “So using that metaphor, I thought about how pebbles carry weight much like our words. Whether they’re negative or positive, words can impact someone.”
“Emanate”, Wyndham Social, 33 Wyndham Street, Central, 11am-9pm daily. Ends December 7.
Like what you read? Follow SCMP Lifestyle on Facebook, X and Instagram. You can also sign up for our eNewsletter here.

data.title: Not a typical footballer: how Inigo Calderon stands out from Hong Kong crowd
data.description:
After Inigo Calderon quit playing football in 2019, he planned to travel the world for a year, indulging his fondness for “going out and feeling free”.
Instead, he ended up in management, and now finds himself in the Hong Kong Premier League, leading Kitchee’s resurgence.
The 43-year-old threw himself into Hong Kong’s mountains and hiking trails to banish initial feelings of being stifled by the weather, crowds and noise, but he has begun generating plenty of commotion of his own.
So ordinary for the past two seasons, Kitchee are top of the table under Calderon.
Calling himself “not a typical footballer”, the Spaniard said he “didn’t have a Porsche or a Ferrari, I had a camper van” during a playing career that peaked during six seasons in England with Brighton & Hove Albion.

But rather than stay on the road, Calderon was tempted back to coach with former club Alaves a month after his retirement. “Things change so much,” he said. “You don’t want to be out of football for too long; you have to take chances when they come.”
That readiness to grasp opportunities led to Calderon accepting the role of Kitchee head coach this summer, two months after his first senior head coach job, at Bristol Rovers, ended in relegation and the sack – and a lesson learned, to be more demanding about player recruitment.
“I was much more involved in signings [with Kitchee] this summer,” he said.
After lasting a mere four months in Bristol, Calderon feared for his future job prospects.
“It was a tough time … it would have been less stressful if I had no family, but I have a wife and two kids and it affects them.” he said.
Calderon had a similarly uncertain period when he was unemployed as a player for six months after leaving Alaves in summer 2009.
“That was the most difficult stage of my career; it affected my personal life,” he said. “But it helped me improve as a person and player.
“I trained with my dog. No one cared if I didn’t set an alarm, but I did every day.”
Gus Poyet signed the tenacious right-back for Brighton, where he became a cult hero as an integral part of a team that grew from League One strugglers to Championship promotion contenders. He left in 2016, a year before Brighton climbed into the Premier League, after making 232 appearances.
“Growing up, I asked my parents for the channels to watch English football – I loved the passion,” Calderon said. “I felt like an English player playing in Spain. It was my dream to play in England, and those were my peak years.”
Born into a football family, with a maternal grandfather, Francisco Zapateria, who was a goalkeeper for Real Zaragoza, Calderon has compiled a document on his laptop detailing the good and the bad from his legion of club bosses. He draws most from Jose Bordalas, his manager at Alicante.
“He was so demanding, even when we were top of our division,” Calderon said. “I saw some bad things from good managers, sometimes not treating players with respect.
“I am demanding, and I shout, but it’s because I want the best for them. If they don’t see that, it’s because they can’t separate the person from the player.”
To “stop my head exploding”, Calderon jots down his daily thoughts. “Ink and think … it keeps me calm and helps me plan,” he said.
Calderon acknowledged, nonetheless, that some players “told me I shout too much” after Kitchee beat Eastern 1-0 in his first match.
“I cannot demand passion then sit with a cigar on the bench. I want the players to see I’m there for them.”
Calderon admits he finds local league games chaotic.
“Lack of control is good for the soul, but as a coach I don’t like it,” he said. “One reason I came here is that the club likes to control games with the ball.”
After his lone defeat, a Senior Shield loss to North District in September, he immediately gathered his players around for a pep talk.

“That five minutes afterwards is an important time … you are hijacked by emotion and have to carefully choose your words, but they are powerful straight after games,” he said. “You learn when emotions are high. I’ve never felt I’ve made a mistake in what I’ve said in those minutes.”
He appreciates the input of Ken Ng Kin, the Kitchee owner, too. “I love that he wants to know things, and that he gives his opinion,” Calderon said. “He sees what we do every day, which gives me confidence that I’ll have time if results aren’t great.”
So far, results are more than adequate.

data.title: From Dior and Chanel, to Aesop and L’Occitane, how hand cream became a year-round staple
data.description:
Every autumn, hand creams quietly make their seasonal return, tucked into handbags and desk drawers as a small but satisfying piece of armour against cold-weather dryness. Colder air, indoor heating and even hard water can strip moisture from the skin faster than you think, making hands one of the first places to feel the change in seasons.

On TikTok, Gen Z and millennials have turned hand care into a ritual that’s part skincare routine, part sensorial indulgence and part lifestyle flex. Feeds are filled with swipes of silky creams, close‑ups of pillowy textures melting into skin, and packaging so chic it practically begs to be photographed. Think Chanel La Crème Main with its glossy egg‑shaped container, or Aesop Aromatique Hand Balm with its sleek amber bottle, each blurring the line between daily essential and design object.
Some of this revival stems from the Covid-19 pandemic, when constant handwashing and sanitising left skin dry and in need of relief. Creams quickly shifted from being used occasionally to becoming an everyday staple, and even as hygiene requirements eased, the habit and comfort they brought remained.

For all the fresh hype, the importance of hand care resonates now for the same reason beauty professionals have warned about for years: hands don’t get the same daily attention we give our faces, and it shows – literally. “Hands will always show the first signs of ageing because they are neglected when it comes to daily care,” says Stephanie G. Laynes, a licensed esthetician based in Texas, and founder of the wax brand Se‑Brazil. “Yes, we wash our hands regularly; however, [most of us] don’t moisturise or protect them on a daily basis.” Laynes notes that even with thousands of hand creams on the market, the real challenge is consistency, as reapplying after every wash sounds simple but is a habit few manage to keep.
It’s a shift fuelled by a larger trend of “skinification” – the application of skincare principles, ingredients and formulations beyond the face. In the case of hand cream, what used to be a utilitarian afterthought is now formulated with the same level of complexity and marketing muscle as a prestige face serum.

And the numbers back it up: Jarsking’s 2025 market analysis shows the hand-care category is far from slowing down, with sales climbing from about US$7.8 billion in 2024 to an estimated US$8.53 billion this year, and on track to reach US$13.3 billion by 2029. Luxury houses like Dior and Chanel continue to hold the crown when it comes to high-end appeal, but newer entrants such as Soft Services Theraplush, with its refillable sculptural vessel and retinol‑infused formula, are capturing attention from skincare purists and TikTok audiences alike.
Las Vegas-based certified dermatologist Dr Jason Stevens notes that today’s hand creams have moved far beyond basic moisturisers. “These days you’ll often find barrier-repairing ceramides, plumping peptides, brightening niacinamide and antioxidant vitamins, all teamed up with heavy-hitting hydrators like shea butter, glycerine and hyaluronic acid,” he says. Some formulas multitask to promote nail health with strengthening keratin or calcium complexes, while others fade sunspots with gentle exfoliating acids, or boost radiance with fermented extracts you’d normally expect in a luxury face mask.

Recent years have also seen a rise in microbiome-friendly blends designed to comfort eczema-prone skin without the sting of essential oils or irritating fragrance. But Laynes warns that not every active-packed formula is a win. “The alpha-hydroxy [acid] with vitamin A combinations can be a little too strong for hands already [experiencing an] imbalance,” she says, explaining that the irritation can be enough to make people stop using them altogether. That’s why, in her own formulations, Laynes goes a different route. Se-Brazil “takes a simple approach by rebalancing the skin with products that can be used on the entire body, with skin-loving oils that will stay on the skin” – a gentler philosophy that focuses on restoring comfort.
Fragrance marks another point of difference. For some, a scented hand cream is an instant mood lifter – a way to wear a favourite perfume in a softer, layered form, as with Byredo, Diptyque or Jo Malone’s matching creams. For others, especially those with reactive skin, even natural fragrance can be a deal breaker. It’s why fragrance-free options now sit alongside their aromatic counterparts on beauty counters, giving people the choice between sensory escape and protecting sensitive skin.

Stevens says knowing your skin type and concerns is key to choosing the right product. If your hands are reactive or prone to eczema, fragrance-free is the safest route. Especially avoid sensitising botanicals that might smell lovely but cause redness or dryness. “If you’re repairing your skin barrier, go for rich occlusives like petrolatum or plant-based butters, and layer them over humectants such as glycerine or hyaluronic acid to help lock in moisture,” he explains. Nécessaire The Hand Cream, often crowned “best overall”, hits that note with a silky texture and fast absorption. Meanwhile, “For sunspots or uneven tone, brightening ingredients like vitamin C, licorice root or niacinamide can help, while lactic acid or urea is great for gently smoothing rough patches,” Stevens adds.

TikTok has also reshaped how and when hand cream gets used. The hand-drenching strategy layers a lightweight lotion with a richer balm or overnight mask for sealed-in hydration; others share knuckle-glow hacks, massaging brightening serums into fingers before cream to mimic fresh manicure sheen. That same layering logic applies off-camera too, and Laynes is a big believer in making it part of your wind-down ritual. “Once you can make the application part of your nighttime routine, [you will see] a fast result without having to reapply as often,” she says. Whether you choose a glow-inducing ritual with Dior’s Le Baume or a nightly slather of L’Occitane’s rich Shea Butter Intensive Hand Balm, the benefits come from the product staying on all night.

Ultimately, Stevens believes hand creams have moved beyond their seasonal niche and into the rhythm of daily life. “People reach for them not just because of what’s inside, but because they look good on a desk, leave a trace of scent on the things you touch, and won’t get in the way of scrolling on your phone,” he says. “These days, the appeal is as much about design and personality as it is about hydration.”

data.title: 123 dead in Sri Lanka after floods, mudslides
data.description:
The death toll from heavy floods and mudslides caused by Cyclone Ditwah in Sri Lanka rose to 123, with 130 people still missing, authorities said Saturday.
Nearly 44,000 people have been displaced and are in temporary shelters, the South Asian country’s disaster management centre said.
The death toll is expected to rise. Social media posts on Saturday showed several areas affected by overnight mudslides that authorities have yet to reach.
Sri Lanka has been battered by severe weather since last week and the conditions worsened on Thursday, with heavy downpours that flooded homes, fields and roads and triggered landslides mainly in the tea-growing central hill country.
The government closed schools and offices and postponed examinations.

Most reservoirs and rivers have overflowed, blocking roads. Authorities stopped passenger trains and closed roads in many parts of the country after rocks, mud and trees fell on roads and railway tracks.
By Friday, water flowing downstream from severely affected areas began to inundate areas around the capital, Colombo, which experienced comparatively less rainfall.
Authorities say that Ditwah, which developed in the seas east of Sri Lanka, is likely to move towards India’s southern coast by Sunday.
Sri Lanka was forecast to see more rain on Saturday, with the storm expected to pass over the northern parts of the country.

data.title: Global stablecoin surge highlights overlooked risks in crypto finance
data.description:
In May 2023, Australian economist Paul Sheard published a book, The Power of Money, explaining how governments and banks create money. Things have moved on since then in the world of cryptocurrency, and it is the little understood international politics of money that need clearing up now.
Public interest continues to centre upon instruments such as bitcoin and its cryptocurrency imitators, whose huge price swings attract headlines. These are not currencies, however, but “risk assets” as Sheard noted during a recent event at the Foreign Correspondents’ Club of Japan in Tokyo.
The focus should be upon developments in the United States, China, Japan and Europe, where the battle for currency domination is accelerating via the issue of stablecoins – a private-sector cryptocurrency whose value is pegged to another asset, typically a fiat currency – and also central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). Both innovations could influence the global balance of currency power as well as domestic economic issues.
Pilot programmes in the use of stablecoins are spreading across the Asia-Pacific. Interest is also growing in CBDCs, with the region becoming a centre for digital payments innovation, Tobias Adrian, director of the International Monetary Fund’s (IMF) monetary and capital markets department, said during a seminar in Tokyo last week.
The politics of cryptocurrencies are intriguing. Bitcoin and other such instruments came into the world with their innovators wishing to bypass the existing system of sovereign currencies because they distrusted governments and central banks. By using blockchain technology to enable fast and cheap financial transactions instead of bank-to-bank exchanges, the belief was that such obstacles could be overcome.
Instead, holders of bitcoin and similar digital assets found that the value of these instruments was wildly volatile, even though their restricted issuance seemed to promise stability. Stablecoins linked to collateral holdings of US dollars and other sovereign securities seemed to provide an answer, even if they shifted control of monetary policy from official authorities to corporate and financial entities.


The fact that stablecoin holdings could be moved more or less freely across national borders alarmed governments and central banks. They responded by devising CBDCs, which are direct liabilities of the issuing central bank and therefore safe, in theory. The various cryptocurrency instruments are now in a competition with the legacy currency system.
The politics around digital currencies have extended into the long-running battle for supremacy between the world’s major currencies. Through legislation and his family’s business activities, US President Donald Trump is promoting the development of US dollar-backed stablecoins to ensure the continued dominance of the US currency. Nine leading European banks have joined forces to launch a euro-denominated stablecoin, which, according to ING, “aims to become a trusted European payment standard in the digital ecosystem”.
Perhaps with an eye towards preventing premature internationalisation of the yuan, the Chinese government has taken a cautious stance on stablecoin issuance while observing Hong Kong’s experiments in this arena. Meanwhile, Japan is taking a more active approach as it views the global spread of the yen as something that should be encouraged.
Japan’s Financial Services Agency announced on November 7 that it would support a pilot project by three major banks – Mizuho, Mitsubishi UFJ and Sumitomo Mitsui – to jointly issue stablecoins. This came shortly after Japanese tech start-up company JPYC launched the world’s first yen-backed stablecoin.


Tommaso Mancini-Griffoli, assistant director of the IMF’s monetary and capital markets department, said during a presentation at the Tokyo seminar that 91 per cent of 93 central banks surveyed last year indicated they were exploring CBDCs. An April 2024 report from a meeting between the Bank of Japan and multiple government ministries noted that “many economies have been studying retail CBDC (general-purpose CBDC) for individual and business users while a few, including the Bahamas, have officially introduced it”.
Interest in stablecoins is surging around the world amid their promise of faster, cheaper payments and a wave of financial innovation. At the same time, however, their spread is accelerating concerns about their effects on global financial systems. In a commentary for Project Syndicate, Angus Armstrong, chief economic adviser at Lloyds Banking Group, and Dennis J. Snower, president emeritus of the Kiel Institute for the World Economy, warn that “stablecoins don’t just disrupt monetary policy; they also reshape fiscal dynamics. Every dollar of stablecoin issuance translates into more demand for government debt. As automatic buyers of Treasury bills, stablecoin issuers are creating a captive market that suppresses yields”.
Meanwhile, the European Systemic Risk Board noted in a report last month that “forceful US policy measures” were supporting the mainstreaming of cryptocurrency assets, including stablecoins. “Amid a fragmented global regulatory landscape, US crypto policies seek to strengthen the dollar’s dominance by promoting the development and adoption of USD-denominated stablecoins worldwide,” the report said.


The report expressed concern about the growing links between the cryptocurrency and financial sectors, underscoring “the urgent need to introduce safeguards against financial stability risks through enhanced supervisory measures, legislative reforms and closer international cooperation”. It singled out stablecoins’ “spillover risks due to their rapid growth, their steadily increasing ties to traditional finance through backing assets and the risks associated with a potential growing role in payment systems”.
The wild swings in value and potential risks to global financial stability have not dampened enthusiasm for cryptocurrency and other digital assets. As such, greater public education on these systems and their risks is necessary before they fully go global.

data.title: China set to benefit as South Africa looks to diversify away from US
data.description:
South Africa is looking to new export markets in Asia – particularly China – and Europe to diversify away from the United States, as its relations with Washington rapidly deteriorate.
America boycotted last weekend’s Group of 20 summit in Johannesburg after Washington accused Pretoria of persecuting Afrikaner farmers, labelling the situation “white genocide” – a claim the South African government strongly denies.
On Wednesday the situation escalated, with US President Donald Trump saying Pretoria would not be invited to next year’s G20 talks in Miami. South Africa called the move “regrettable” and based on “misinformation and distortions” and said it had a right to attend as a sovereign, founding member of the G20.
It comes two months after a US trade initiative with Africa – the African Growth and Opportunity Act – expired, ending duty-free access for many South African exports.
In addition, a 30 per cent tariff imposed on South African exports by the US has meanwhile severely hurt farms, vehicle manufacturers and car parts makers, as well as mining companies.

South Africa is responding by pursuing new markets in Asia and Europe to diversify exports and attract investment.
According to analysts, the pivot can be seen in South Africa’s diplomatic activities in countries like China, Vietnam and Indonesia, as well as in the European Union, with efforts under way to secure investment and new market access for South African goods, particularly in manufacturing and agriculture.
The analysts said China was emerging as the main beneficiary as it stood to deepen economic and political ties with South Africa, while the US was at risk of ceding influence across the African continent.
Tim Zajontz, interim professor in International Relations at the University of Freiburg, said the South African government was seeking to further diversify its political and economic ties.
Pretoria aimed to redirect some of its exports – especially car parts, other manufactured goods and agricultural produce – to other destinations through a combination of subsidies, trade facilitation measures and economic diplomacy, according to Zajontz.
He said the efforts were being directed towards Europe, the Gulf states and various Asian and Latin American countries.
“The estrangement from the US will further intensify economic and political cooperation between South Africa and China,” Zajontz added.
To cultivate new economic allies in Asia, a South African delegation has visited China, Vietnam and Indonesia. President Cyril Ramaphosa also took part in the EU’s Global Gateway Forum in Brussels in October and recently visited Ireland and Switzerland in pursuit of investors and new markets.
Chrispin Phiri, spokesman for South Africa’s international relations minister, confirmed the nation was intensifying efforts to diversify its markets.
He acknowledged that the US was a substantial market for South Africa but said it was “determined to pursue economic diplomacy in earnest” in a bid to ramp up trade with other places.
One of those is China. During a meeting with South African leaders on the sidelines of the G20 summit, Chinese Premier Li Qiang said Beijing was willing to align development strategies, accelerate zero-tariff measures, and deepen cooperation in sectors like mining, infrastructure, cars and new energy.
He also pledged to deepen political mutual trust, firmly support South Africa’s core interests, and strengthen cooperation within platforms like Brics and the G20.

Zajontz, who is also a research fellow with the Centre for International and Comparative Politics at Stellenbosch University, said that in contrast with the US, China was opening its vast market to African goods and services.
“We are also going to see additional Chinese investments in South Africa’s automotive industry as well as in transport, energy and digital infrastructure,” he said.
Meanwhile, the Clean Trade and Investment Partnership signed between South Africa and Europe secures new market access and significant investment for South Africa’s green economy transition. The deal focuses on promoting battery manufacturing and facilitating South Africa’s export of electric vehicles to the EU market.
South Africa’s strained US relations are part of a broader pattern. Ties with Nigeria were similarly frayed after Trump threatened military action over what he claimed was the government’s failure to prevent widespread killings of Christians – allegations Abuja rejected.
According to John Calabrese, a non-resident senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, Washington’s absence from the G20 allowed Beijing to project itself as a reliable partner, strengthen economic ties and reinforce its narrative as Africa’s “go-to ally”.
He said the move reinforced incentives for African nations to lean into Brics and Chinese investment and signalled that US support was neither automatic nor guaranteed.
Calabrese said the move was shortsighted and risked the erosion of American influence while reinforcing China’s foothold in Africa.
China has gained influence by providing needed infrastructure across the continent, according to Professor John Kirton, director of the G20 Research Group at the University of Toronto.
“[For supply chains] you really need ports and railroads – and Africa needs a lot more of that still,” he added.

data.title: In ‘Schrodinger’s Pakistan’, the young feel sidelined by their own state
data.description:
In Pakistan, a nation where power often seems to serve itself above all else, the latest constitutional overhaul has been received with a weary sense of resignation.
Pushed through parliament on November 13 with barely a murmur of opposition, the 27th amendment to the country’s 1973 constitution made two major changes: splitting the Supreme Court into separate appellate and constitutional benches, and upgrading the army chief of staff to overall commander of Pakistan’s armed forces.
Viewed in isolation, both moves have plausible justifications. Pakistan’s higher judiciary has been swamped by cases for decades – a backlog compounded by activist judges who, since the restoration of democracy in 2008, have devoted extraordinary time to politically charged hearings.
Meanwhile, the government had argued it needed a more integrated command structure to meet the demands of modern warfare following a brief but deadly aerial conflict with India in May, replacing the largely ceremonial chairman of the joint chiefs with a hands-on chief of defence forces.

Seen against Pakistan’s longer political history, however, the amendments are more calculated power grabs than practical governance changes.
With its commanding parliamentary majority, Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif’s multiparty coalition has seized an opportunity to neutralise an unpredictable Supreme Court whose populist, partisan interventions have repeatedly destabilised elected governments, while simultaneously appeasing the armed forces’ appetite for control.
Pakistan’s top judges have repeatedly thrown their weight into the political arena in recent years, toppling prime ministers and acting as bulwarks for favoured leaders. Both Yusuf Raza Gilani in 2012 and Nawaz Sharif in 2017 were removed from office on dubious legal grounds, while a former chief justice was accused of giving special treatment to Imran Khan, who is currently serving multiple prison sentences including 14 years for corruption.

By bifurcating the Supreme Court, Sharif’s coalition is also widely seen as doing the military’s bidding. Pakistan’s generals have made little secret of their desire to keep Khan behind bars until he falls into line, as much of the country’s political class already has.
True to his combative persona, Khan has refused to yield, apparently banking on a mix of mass popularity and patience to eventually topple what critics call Pakistan’s “hybrid democracy” – a system in which elected leaders and the military govern in tandem, but with the generals ultimately retaining veto power.
‘Schrodinger’s Pakistan’
Across Pakistan, the amendments have landed with a thud. Seven Pakistanis interviewed by This Week In Asia – men and women aged 22 to 51 from the capital Islamabad, the eastern metropolis of Lahore and farming communities in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and Punjab – expressed varying degrees of disillusionment.
“There seems to be a quiet power struggle between the parliament, the military and the judiciary,” said Ajalan Ali, 24, an Islamabad resident who recently graduated from a Turkish university with a degree in international relations and political science. “Rather than coexisting, each entity seems to be struggling to expand its control over Pakistan.”

For Marukh Khalid, a 22-year-old undergraduate at Lahore University of Management Sciences, the problem is broader than any single amendment. “All institutions in Pakistan have become increasingly self-serving and have failed to do much of anything for the common people,” she said.
“Each institution ultimately tries to be the most powerful of the powerful, while the rest of the country is forced to watch, and we see that with the 27th amendment.”
From her vantage point at Lahore’s Forman Christian College University, where she manages research projects and public relations, 30-year-old Ayesha Siddiqua sees in the reforms an unvarnished display of power.
“These institutions have now openly shown the people of Pakistan how things run and who runs them,” she said, noting a “drastic change” since Khan was toppled after the military withdrew its support.
“Instead of saving face, they [the generals] have made themselves more powerful by forcing their hand on politicians, the judiciary and parliament, giving them no other choice but to comply with them.”

In her view, that might bring a perverse upside. The arrangement “might be good to some extent since it may lead to stability in the government at least,” she said.
For Aneesah Amer Shah, the 27th amendment is not a cure for institutional decay but its symptom. All three branches of the state – the executive, the legislature and the judiciary – “have failed to perform their constitutional duties and the 27th amendment is a clear reflection of that failure”, said the 24-year-old architect from Swat.
From her small Basmati rice farm along the banks of the River Chenab, 51-year-old Rehana Afzal has reached a similar conclusion. “Every institution is showing a decline in its performance,” she said. “Most are just supporting the military’s vision for the country.”
Others see the hybrid system, in which civilian and military leaders share power uneasily, as an unpleasant but necessary compromise. “For the time being, hybrid democracy is reality,” said Syed Taimur Jan, a member of Khan’s Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf party from the mountainous district of Dir, bordering Afghanistan. “But it won’t sustain for long.”

Self-described “realist” Nibaat Hassan Syed, 24, who is pursuing a master’s degree in political science and whose brother is training to be an army officer, stressed that democracy in Pakistan had “always existed alongside the state institutions that play a stabilising role”.
“Many people feel that, given Pakistan’s unique security concerns, a mixed model where civilian leadership and strong institutions work together provides continuity and order,” she said.
Rather than labelling it hybrid, Syed prefers to frame Pakistan’s system as a “pragmatic” arrangement designed “to keep the country functioning during political instability”.
Those interviewed by This Week in Asia were just as divided about the country’s trajectory in the years ahead.
Siddiqua in Lahore is cautiously hopeful. She expected “things to get well, [though] there might not be an absolute democracy any time soon in Pakistan”. If the hybrid order delivered basic governance, “I am OK with it”, she said.

Khalid, the undergraduate student, fears the opposite. To her, “this is just the beginning of the end of democracy in Pakistan”.
“Critical issues will be disregarded, and power and wealth will remain in the hands of the few,” she warned, predicting the country was “going to become more unsafe than it already is, especially for anyone with an opinion”.
Ali, the recent graduate in Islamabad, was more upbeat, arguing that Pakistan has a knack for defying expectations – for better and for worse.
“This is Schrodinger’s Pakistan,” he quipped – in a nod to the famous thought experiment in which a cat in a box is considered both dead and alive until observed – noting that just a year ago “no one would believe” that by 2025, Pakistan would see its leaders welcomed at the White House, sign a mutual defence pact with Saudi Arabia, shoot down attacking Indian warplanes and begin purging Afghanistan-based terrorist groups from its territory.
Looking to the Pakistan Stock Exchange for inspiration, Ali said, “I see Pakistan going a bit down before rising up – akin to a J-curve”.
